citation: Hecht et al. (2025) - Rocks and walls: Biodiversity and temperature regulation of natural cliffs and vertical greenery systems
description: Section-skeleton few-shot example for design strategy extraction. Blue-highlighted passages from the annotated PDF are preserved as relevant_snippets.
section_skeleton:
Paper structure (Hecht et al., 2025 — 16 pages, ~68,000 characters):
--- TITLE & ABSTRACT (page 1) ---
"Vertical greenery systems (VGSs) have been proposed to mitigate rising temperatures and the loss of biodiversity."
"air and surface temperature measurements as well as biodiversity observations were conducted on two different types of existing VGSs (climber, foliage) on buildings in Singapore and compared to non-vegetated building walls and vegetated natural cliffs."
--- INTRODUCTION (pages 1-2) ---
[...background on urban heat island, VGSs, temperature regulation, biodiversity — IGNORE THIS]
--- METHODOLOGY (pages 2-5) --- ← LOOK HERE
"The study took place in the urban environment of Singapore between August 2022 and March 2023."
[...describes types of VGSs studied, measurement methods, biodiversity data collection...]
"These VGSs are largely of two types – climber and foliage."
"Climber VGSs consist of self-climbing plants such as ivy or vine species that grow from the soil up on a structure typically wires or cables."
"Foliage VGSs typically consist of smaller plants such as Philodendron species that are potted in boxes and that are generally managed using an integrated irrigation system."
--- RESULTS (pages 5-10) ---
[...air and surface temperature data, biodiversity data — THESE ARE ECOSYSTEM SERVICES NOT DESIGN STRATEGIES]
--- DISCUSSION (pages 10-14) ---
[...analysis of temperature performance, biodiversity benefits — IGNORE THIS]
--- CONCLUSIONS (pages 14-15) ---
[...summary of findings — IGNORE THIS]

relevant_snippets.text: air and surface temperature measurements as well as biodiversity observations were conducted on two different types of existing VGSs (climber, foliage) on buildings in Singapore and compared to non-vegetated building walls and vegetated natural cliffs.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Identifies the design strategy: vertical greenery systems in different wall types including climber, foliage, and comparison with natural cliffs and non-vegetated controls

relevant_snippets.text: Foliage VGSs typically consist of smaller plants such as Philodendron species that are potted in boxes and that are generally managed using an integrated irrigation system.
Climber VGSs consist of self-climbing plants such as ivy or vine species that grow from the soil up on a structure typically wires or cables.
Rocky cliffs were included as a 'natural reference' (hereafter called natural cliffs) to set a benchmark and identify the extent to which man-made VGSs
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Implementation detail: natural cliffs used as reference for VGS performance

relevant_snippets.text: each climber and foliage VGS was paired with a control wall within 200 m distance and exposed to the same cardinal direction
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Implementation detail: controlled experimental setup with non-vegetated walls

design_strategies.name: Vertical green systems in walls
design_strategies.anchor: two different types of existing VGSs (climber, foliage) on buildings
implementation_details: Foliage VGSs typically consist of smaller plants such as Philodendron species
Climber VGSs consist of self-climbing plants such as ivy or vine species
Rocky cliffs were included as a 'natural reference'
each climber and foliage VGS was paired with a control wall

lesson: From this 16-page paper, the design strategy is vertical greenery systems (VGSs) implemented on building walls. The study compares two VGS types (foliage and climber) against natural cliffs and non-vegetated control walls. Implementation details describe how each type works: foliage with potted plants and integrated irrigation, climbers growing from soil up on wires/cables, and natural cliffs as a benchmark. The model should identify the VGS as the design strategy and not confuse the measured outcomes (temperature regulation, biodiversity) as design strategies — those are ecosystem services.
